rows.v: The 'Stars and Stripes' features 13 horizontal red and white stripes and a blue canton with 50 white stars.
The 13 stripes represent the original colonies, while each of the 50 stars stands for a current state.
The design has been modified 27 times as states joined; the current 50-star version, designed in part by a teenager for a school project, dates to 1960.
The 'Maple Leaf' flag features a single red, 11-pointed maple leaf centered on a white square between two red bands.
The maple leaf is a longstanding national symbol of Canada, and the red-and-white colors were declared the national colors in 1921.
Adopted only in 1965 after fierce debate, it replaced flags bearing the British Union Jack, marking a milestone in Canada's distinct national identity.
A vertical tricolor of green, white, and red, with the national coat of arms centered on the white band.
The emblem depicts an eagle devouring a serpent atop a cactus, fulfilling an Aztec prophecy about where to found their capital.
The legend guided the Aztecs to build Tenochtitlan, today's Mexico City, making the flag a direct link to the nation's pre-colonial roots.
A green field with a yellow rhombus, a blue celestial globe, and a white banner reading 'Ordem e Progresso.'
The motto means 'Order and Progress,' and the stars depict the sky over Rio de Janeiro on a specific date in 1889.
The 27 stars represent the states and federal district; the sky shown is that of the morning the republic was proclaimed, frozen in the flag forever.
Three horizontal bands of light blue, white, and light blue, with a golden 'Sun of May' centered on the white.
The Sun of May commemorates the May Revolution of 1810 that began Argentina's path to independence.
The sky-blue and white colors are traditionally linked to the cockades worn by patriots, and the radiant sun is an emblem also found on Uruguay's flag.
A gold saltire (diagonal cross) dividing the flag into green triangles at top and bottom and black triangles at the sides.
Jamaica's is the only national flag in the world that contains none of the colors red, white, or blue.
Adopted at independence in 1962, its colors symbolize the strength and creativity of the people (black), the land and hope (green), and sunshine (gold).
The 'Union Jack' combines the crosses of three patron saints into a single overlapping red, white, and blue design.
It merges the crosses of St. George (England), St. Andrew (Scotland), and St. Patrick (Ireland) into one flag.
Its layered design reflects the political union of the kingdoms, and it appears within the flags of many former British territories and Commonwealth nations.
The 'Tricolore' consists of three equal vertical bands of blue, white, and red.
Born of the French Revolution, its colors are often linked to liberty, equality, and fraternity, and to the city of Paris and the monarchy.
One of the most influential flag designs in history, its revolutionary tricolor pattern inspired the national flags of countless other nations.
Three horizontal bands of black, red, and gold.
The colors trace to 19th-century movements for German unity and democracy, and to the uniforms of volunteer soldiers fighting Napoleon.
Banned and revived through Germany's turbulent history, the black-red-gold tricolor today stands specifically for democratic and republican tradition.
A vertical tricolor of green, white, and red, inspired by the French Tricolore.
Adopted by revolutionaries seeking Italian unification, the design echoes the French flag that inspired Europe's age of nationalism.
First used in the 1790s, it became the flag of a unified Italy in the 19th century; popular legend ties its colors to the landscape and faith.
Two horizontal red bands framing a wider yellow band, with the national coat of arms set toward the hoist side.
The coat of arms displays the heraldic symbols of Spain's historic kingdoms and the Pillars of Hercules.
The pillars bear the motto 'Plus Ultra' (Further Beyond), a reference to Spanish exploration past the edge of the once-known world.
A red square — one of only two square national flags — bearing a bold white cross at its center.
The white cross on red is an ancient symbol, and Switzerland's flag inspired the emblem of the Red Cross in reverse colors.
The humanitarian Red Cross, founded in Geneva, honored its host nation by inverting the Swiss flag's colors for its now-universal symbol.
Nine horizontal blue and white stripes with a white cross on a blue canton in the upper corner.
The cross represents Greek Orthodox Christianity, and the nine stripes are popularly linked to the syllables of the independence motto.
The motto, 'Eleftheria i Thanatos' (Freedom or Death), drove the Greek War of Independence; blue and white have evoked sea and sky for generations.
A unique design featuring a horizontal 'Y' shape in green that converges the flag's six colors.
Adopted in 1994 at the end of apartheid, the converging 'Y' symbolizes the unification of diverse elements into one nation.
It incorporates colors from earlier flags and liberation movements, and was the first national flag designed in the post-apartheid democratic era.
Horizontal bands of black, red, and green separated by thin white stripes, with a Maasai shield and spears at the center.
The shield and spears symbolize the defense of freedom, while black, red, and green represent the people, struggle, and land.
The Maasai warrior's shield at the heart of the flag honors the nation's defense of the hard-won independence achieved in 1963.
Three horizontal bands of red, white, and black, with the golden 'Eagle of Saladin' centered on the white.
The Eagle of Saladin is a symbol of Arab nationalism and a medieval Muslim leader, shared with several other Arab nations' heraldry.
The red-white-black tricolor is a pan-Arab pattern, and the eagle ties modern Egypt to the legendary 12th-century commander Saladin.
Three vertical bands, with green on the outer edges framing a white band in the center.
The green represents Nigeria's agriculture and forests, while the white symbolizes peace and unity.
Chosen through a 1959 design contest won by a student, its simple, striking design has represented the nation since independence in 1960.
Three horizontal bands of red, gold, and green with a black five-pointed star centered on the gold band.
The black 'Lodestar of African Freedom' is a symbol of African emancipation, inspiring liberation movements across the continent.
Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African nation to gain independence, in 1957, and its pioneering flag influenced many that followed.
Horizontal bands of green, yellow, and red with a blue disc bearing a golden star and rays at the center.
Ethiopia's green-yellow-red combination became the basis for the Pan-African colors adopted across the continent.
As one of the few African nations never colonized, its ancient flag became a powerful emblem of African independence and pride worldwide.
A white field with a single large red disc in the center, representing the sun.
Called the 'Hinomaru' (circle of the sun), it reflects Japan's name as the 'Land of the Rising Sun.'
The sun holds deep significance in Japanese tradition and Shinto belief, and the elegant, minimalist design is among the world's most recognizable.
A red field with a large golden star and four smaller golden stars arranged in an arc in the upper hoist corner.
The large star represents the Communist Party, and the four smaller stars the united people of the nation.
Adopted in 1949 at the founding of the People's Republic, the red field symbolizes the communist revolution that brought it to power.
A horizontal tricolor of saffron, white, and green, with a navy-blue 24-spoked wheel centered on the white band.
The wheel, the 'Ashoka Chakra,' represents the eternal wheel of law and dharma from ancient Indian tradition.
The saffron, white, and green are often read as courage, peace, and faith, and the ancient chakra ties modern India to its philosophical heritage.
A white field with a red-and-blue 'taegeuk' yin-yang symbol at the center, framed by four black trigrams.
The taegeuk represents cosmic balance, while the four trigrams symbolize the elements and principles of the universe.
Drawn from East Asian philosophy and the I Ching, the 'Taegukgi' expresses harmony and balance, a meditative emblem unique among national flags.
A red field bearing a white crescent moon and a five-pointed star.
The star and crescent are longstanding symbols associated with the Ottoman Empire and the wider Islamic world.
Inherited from the Ottoman Empire, the design has become one of the most widely recognized symbols associated with Turkey and Muslim heritage.
A white field with two horizontal blue stripes and a blue Star of David centered between them.
The Star of David is an ancient symbol of Jewish identity, and the design evokes the traditional Jewish prayer shawl.
The blue stripes recall the 'tallit,' the prayer shawl, linking the modern state's flag directly to centuries of Jewish religious tradition.
A blue field with the Union Jack in the canton, a large Commonwealth Star, and the Southern Cross constellation.
The Southern Cross is a constellation visible in the Southern Hemisphere, and the large star represents the federation of states.
The Union Jack reflects British heritage, while the Southern Cross has long served as a navigational and symbolic marker of the southern skies.
A blue field with the Union Jack in the canton and four red, white-bordered stars forming the Southern Cross.
Like Australia's, it features the Southern Cross, but with four red stars rather than the larger constellation and federation star.
A 2016 national referendum considered replacing it with a silver-fern design, but voters chose to keep the historic flag.
The world's only non-rectangular national flag, formed by two stacked crimson pennants with a blue border.
Nepal's flag is the only national flag that is not a quadrilateral, its shape derived from two combined triangular pennants.
The two pennants represent the Himalayas and two branches of the royal and political past; the moon and sun symbols express a wish for the nation's longevity.
A red field with a single large five-pointed golden star at its center.
The red symbolizes revolution and bloodshed, while the gold star's five points represent the classes united in building the nation.
Known as the 'red flag with a gold star,' it emerged from the communist independence movement and was adopted upon reunification.
Five horizontal bands of red, white, blue, white, and red, with the central blue band twice the height of the others.
The colors stand for the nation (red), religion (white), and the monarchy (blue), the three pillars of Thai identity.
The prominent central blue band, added in 1917, was also a gesture of solidarity with allies who shared red-white-blue flags during World War I.
